syllabus.title: The World of Homer
syllabus.content: What kind of culture produced the Iliad, and what did heroism mean before philosophy could define it?

syllabus.title: Achilles and the Moral Crisis
syllabus.content: Why Achilles withdraws, what he discovers, and why his greatness cannot be reduced to rage.

syllabus.title: Hektor, Troy, and Mortal Love
syllabus.content: The poem’s vision of family, city, duty, and the tragic nobility of Hektor.

syllabus.title: The Ending of the Iliad
syllabus.content: Priam, Achilles, pity, reverence, and the final meaning of the poem.

book_note: Now enrolling for the next live cohort.
art_alt: Achilles artwork for the Iliad course
quote_text: The classics endure because they dramatize truths about life, morality, and the human spirit that remain inexhaustible.
subtitle: An online course on wrath, honor, mortality, and greatness.
description: Homer’s Iliad is not merely the story of Achilles’ anger. It is a poem about what men live for, what they are willing to die for, and what kind of greatness can survive the knowledge of mortality.
